posts.title: AI agents are the new CI/CD
posts.hook: CI/CD didn't replace developers. It replaced the 45 minutes you spent manually deploying every Friday afternoon.
posts.body:
CI/CD didn't replace developers. It replaced the 45 minutes you spent manually deploying every Friday afternoon.

AI agents are doing the same thing to GTM.

I run 50+ AI skills across my monorepo. They don't replace me. They replace the repetitive loops I used to burn hours on.

Account research that took 30 minutes per prospect? Automated into a Clay + Exa enrichment flow that runs while I sleep.

Content scheduling across 4 websites? A cron job on my Mac Mini handles it at midnight.

Lead scoring that lived in my head? Now it's a Python script that queries Apollo, scores against my ICP, and pushes qualified accounts to Attio.

The pattern is identical to what happened with CI/CD. Nobody argued Jenkins was replacing engineers. It was obviously infrastructure. But with AI agents, people keep framing it as replacement instead of what it actually is... tooling.

The companies winning right now aren't asking "will AI replace my SDRs." They're asking "what does my SDR do 40 times a day that a script could do better."

That's the question worth answering.
posts.cta: What's the most repetitive loop in your GTM workflow right now?

inspired_by.author: Nat Friedman
inspired_by.summary: Nat reflects on the explosion of AI agents in software development, comparing it to the rise of CI/CD pipelines. He discusses infrastructure bottlenecks and recommends tools like Ray for distributed agent training. Stresses the need for open-source automation frameworks.

posts.title: SDRs make the best GTM engineers
posts.hook: I used to send 200+ cold emails a day from primary domains with no warmup on SalesLoft. No dedicated IP. No bounce protection. Just vibes and a quota.
posts.body:
I used to send 200+ cold emails a day from primary domains with no warmup on SalesLoft. No dedicated IP. No bounce protection. Just vibes and a quota.

That experience is the reason I can build GTM infrastructure now.

When I write a Clay enrichment table, I know what bad data looks like because I manually built buying committees in Salesforce for two years. When I set up domain rotation in Instantly, I understand deliverability because I burned domains firsthand.

The people building the best outbound automation aren't coming from engineering backgrounds. They're coming from the SDR floor. They know which parts of the workflow are soul-crushing busywork and which parts actually require human judgment.

RevOps teams are hiring engineers to build pipelines they don't fully understand. Meanwhile, former SDRs who learned to code are building systems that actually reflect how outbound works in practice.

The gap isn't technical skill. It's context. You can teach an SDR Python in 6 months. You can't teach an engineer what it feels like to get ghosted on follow-up 47.
posts.cta: If you came up through sales and started building, I want to hear your story in the comments.

inspired_by.author: Sami Rahman
inspired_by.summary: Discusses building scalable GTM engines by integrating engineering with sales processes. Shares a framework for automating outbound at scale while maintaining personalization. Emphasizes data-driven iteration for revenue growth.

posts.title: Multi-agent systems in production
posts.hook: Everyone's talking about multi-agent systems. Most people running them in production will tell you the same thing... the hard part isn't the agents. It's the coordination.
posts.body:
Everyone's talking about multi-agent systems. Most people running them in production will tell you the same thing... the hard part isn't the agents. It's the coordination.

I run agent teams across my monorepo. Research agents, content agents, data enrichment agents. Each one works fine in isolation. The chaos starts when they need to share context.

Three things I learned the hard way:

1. Every agent needs a constraint file. Mine live in `.claude/teams/TEAM-CONSTRAINTS.md`. Without explicit boundaries, agents duplicate work or step on each other's output.

2. Handoff protocols matter more than agent capability. I built a handoff system where each agent writes a context doc on exit so the next session picks up cleanly. No handoff = no continuity = wasted compute.

3. Route before you spawn. I have a routing skill that decides whether a task needs a team, a subagent, or a single focused session. Most tasks don't need three agents. They need one agent with good context.

The multi-agent future is real. But it looks less like a swarm of autonomous bots and more like a well-organized engineering team with clear ownership and communication protocols.
posts.cta: What's your biggest coordination challenge running agents in production?

inspired_by.author: Swyx
inspired_by.summary: Swyx discusses the evolution of AI agents, emphasizing multi-agent systems and their role in automating complex developer workflows. He highlights key challenges in reliability and orchestration, sharing insights from recent experiments with tools like LangGraph. The post calls for better developer tools to scale AI infrastructure.

posts.title: Building in public from a monorepo
posts.hook: I ship from a single monorepo. 4 live websites, 50+ AI skills, a CRM integration layer, content pipelines, and a full GTM engine. All in one repo.
posts.body:
I ship from a single monorepo. 4 live websites, 50+ AI skills, a CRM integration layer, content pipelines, and a full GTM engine. All in one repo.

People ask why I don't split it up. The answer is simple. Context.

When my blog generator cron runs at midnight, it needs access to SEO data, voice rules, the website build system, and the deployment pipeline. If those lived in 5 repos, I'd spend more time wiring them together than building features.

My stack: TypeScript for the web layer (Next.js, React, Remotion). Python for scripting and data pipelines. A Mac Mini running 24/7 as the cron server. Claude Code as the primary dev tool.

Last week I shipped a landing page generator that researches a prospect's company, builds a custom branded page, and deploys it to a /for/company-name route. The whole flow touches SEO data, voice normalization, the Next.js build, and Cloudflare deployment. In a monorepo, that's one PR. In a polyrepo world, that's four PRs and a prayer.

Solo builders don't need microservices. They need everything within arm's reach.
posts.cta: Monorepo or polyrepo for solo builders? I'll die on this hill but curious where you land.

inspired_by.author: Pieter Levels
inspired_by.summary: Pieter shares his journey as a solo developer shipping multiple products like Nomad List and Remote OK without VC funding. He emphasizes the power of building in public, iterating based on user feedback, and achieving profitability through bootstrapping. His post details the latest product launch and metrics achieved in the first week.

posts.title: RevOps is not GTM engineering
posts.hook: RevOps and GTM engineering are not the same thing. One configures tools. The other builds systems.
posts.body:
RevOps and GTM engineering are not the same thing. One configures tools. The other builds systems.

RevOps: sets up Salesforce fields, builds HubSpot workflows, manages Outreach sequences, pulls reports. Essential work. Keeps the lights on.

GTM engineering: writes the Python script that enriches 500 accounts overnight through Clay and Apollo, scores them against a custom ICP model, pushes qualified ones to Attio with full buying committee data attached, and triggers a personalized sequence. No human touched it.

The difference isn't prestige. It's leverage.

A RevOps person can optimize your existing funnel. A GTM engineer can build a new one from scratch that runs while the team sleeps.

I've been both. I manually configured SalesLoft cadences for months before I wrote my first enrichment script. That foundation matters. You need to understand the workflow before you can automate it.

But the ceiling is different. RevOps scales linearly with headcount. GTM engineering scales with compute.

The companies figuring this out are the ones quietly building engineering functions inside their go-to-market teams. Not replacing RevOps. Extending it with code.
posts.cta: Does your GTM team have anyone who can write code, or is it all no-code tooling?

inspired_by.author: Dan Tyre
inspired_by.summary: Breaks down revenue operations as the backbone of modern GTM strategies. Covers integrating sales ops with outbound automation for predictable scaling. Includes case study from HubSpot's playbook.

posts.title: Browser automation for GTM
posts.hook: Browser automation isn't just for QA anymore. I use Playwright to run GTM workflows that no API supports.
posts.body:
Browser automation isn't just for QA anymore. I use Playwright to run GTM workflows that no API supports.

Some platforms don't have APIs. Some have APIs that are missing critical endpoints. Some rate-limit you into oblivion. The browser doesn't care about any of that.

I have Playwright scripts that:

➡️ Scout Reddit threads for relevant conversations and log them to a queue
➡️ Pull LinkedIn company data that isn't available through any official API
➡️ Monitor competitor pricing pages for changes
➡️ Capture screenshots of prospect websites for personalized outreach assets

The key lesson: reuse existing browser sessions. Don't spawn fresh contexts that wipe your logins. I burned hours debugging auth flows before I learned to just persist the session state.

The other key lesson: browser automation is fragile. Selectors break. Pages change layouts. Rate limits still exist, they're just less visible. Build your scripts to fail gracefully and alert you instead of silently producing garbage data.

WebGPU and local inference are making this even more interesting. Running a small model locally to parse page content means you don't need to send every scraped page to an API.

The browser is an underrated GTM tool. Most people just use it to check LinkedIn.
posts.cta: Anyone else using browser automation for GTM workflows? Drop your use case.

inspired_by.author: Addy Osmani
inspired_by.summary: Osmani breaks down developer tools for building AI agents, focusing on browser-based automation and WebGPU for local inference. He shares benchmarks on AI infrastructure performance and tips for integrating agents into web dev stacks. Advocates for standardized APIs to accelerate adoption.

posts.title: The plumber to programmer pipeline
posts.hook: I was a plumber before I was an SDR. Before that, I was doing manual labor in Arizona heat.
posts.body:
I was a plumber before I was an SDR. Before that, I was doing manual labor in Arizona heat.

Now I build AI-native GTM infrastructure from a monorepo with 50+ automation skills.

The path doesn't make sense on paper. It makes perfect sense in practice.

Plumbing taught me systems thinking. Water flows downhill, pressure matters, every joint is a potential failure point. Software works the same way. Data flows through pipelines, bottlenecks kill throughput, every integration is a potential failure point.

SDR work taught me the pain. When you've manually researched 50 accounts in a day, built buying committees by hand in Salesforce, and sent hundreds of emails from a domain you're actively destroying... you know exactly what to automate first.

The career pivot crowd on LinkedIn makes it sound like a clean story. It wasn't. I taught myself Python by building scripts that solved problems I had at work. No bootcamp. No CS degree. Just a specific problem and Stack Overflow.

The best builders I know didn't follow a traditional path. They came from trades, sales, ops, support. They understand the work because they did the work.

Your background isn't a liability. It's your competitive advantage.
posts.cta: What's your non-traditional path into tech? The weirder the better.

inspired_by.author: Sahil Lavingia
inspired_by.summary: Sahil discusses pivoting from a high-flying VC-backed startup to bootstrapping Gumroad as a solo founder initially. He reflects on career lessons from the pivot into sustainable tech entrepreneurship and building products for creators. The post inspires those considering tech career shifts.

posts.title: AI coding agents changed my workflow
posts.hook: 6 months ago I wrote every line of code myself. Now Claude Code writes 70% of my first drafts and I spend my time reviewing, directing, and architecting.
posts.body:
6 months ago I wrote every line of code myself. Now Claude Code writes 70% of my first drafts and I spend my time reviewing, directing, and architecting.

This isn't a productivity hack post. It's a workflow shift that changed what I can build as a solo operator.

My setup: Claude Code on a Max subscription running Opus. I give it plan mode for anything non-trivial. It explores the codebase, writes a spec, I approve or redirect, then it builds. I review the diff like a senior engineer reviewing a junior's PR.

What changed:

➡️ I shipped a full landing page generator in one session. Company research, custom branding, motion design, deployment. Would have taken me a week solo.

➡️ My cron pipeline (blog generation, SEO optimization, social scheduling) was built in days instead of weeks.

➡️ I maintain 4 websites, a CRM integration, and a GTM engine. As one person.

The shift isn't "AI writes code for me." It's "I operate at a higher level of abstraction." I think in systems and flows. The agent handles implementation details.

The catch: you need to be good enough to review the output. AI coding agents amplify skill. They don't replace it. If you can't spot a bad architecture decision in a diff, the agent will happily build the wrong thing fast.
posts.cta: How has AI coding tooling changed your build velocity? Genuinely curious about other solo builders' experiences.

inspired_by.author: Andrej Karpathy
inspired_by.summary: Karpathy shares a deep dive into building production-grade AI agents for coding tasks, focusing on automation in developer environments. He outlines infrastructure needs like efficient token streaming and error recovery mechanisms. Emphasizes the shift from LLMs to agentic systems in dev tools.

posts.title: Content as GTM infrastructure
posts.hook: Most companies treat content as marketing. I treat it as infrastructure.
posts.body:
Most companies treat content as marketing. I treat it as infrastructure.

My content pipeline is a cron job. Every night at midnight, my Mac Mini runs a script that:

1. Pulls trending topics from my SEO tracking data
2. Cross-references against my keyword log and used-keywords list to avoid repetition
3. Generates drafts using Claude Opus with my voice system loaded (core voice rules, anti-slop filters, platform-specific playbooks)
4. Runs each draft through a 5-gate improvement protocol
5. Queues approved posts to Typefully for scheduled publishing

The voice system alone has 29 anti-pattern rules. If a draft triggers 3+ flags, it gets rewritten from scratch, not patched.

This isn't content at scale. It's content as a system. The difference matters.

Content at scale means more volume. Content as a system means consistent quality, zero manual scheduling, built-in brand safety, and SEO coverage that compounds over time.

I publish across 4 sites. Blog posts, social content, landing pages. All flowing from the same voice DNA, the same keyword strategy, the same cron infrastructure.

Bootstrapped SaaS founders spending 10 hours a week on content... that time is better spent building the system that does it for you.
posts.cta: Is your content workflow manual, semi-automated, or fully pipelined? No judgment, just mapping the landscape.

inspired_by.author: Arvid Kahl
inspired_by.summary: Outlines a content strategy for bootstrapped SaaS companies focusing on GTM motions. Details using outbound automation tools ethically to amplify content reach. Shares metrics from his own experiments.

posts.title: Ship small bets from one machine
posts.hook: Two machines. One monorepo. Four live websites. Zero employees.
posts.body:
Two machines. One monorepo. Four live websites. Zero employees.

My MacBook Pro is the dev machine. My Mac Mini is the always-on server. Between them, they run my entire operation.

The Mac Mini handles:
➡️ Nightly cron jobs (content generation, SEO optimization, data enrichment)
➡️ A local Qwen 2.5 14B model on Ollama for high-frequency tasks that would cost too much on API
➡️ Git sync scripts that keep everything current
➡️ Reddit and social media monitoring

The MacBook handles:
➡️ All development work
➡️ Claude Code sessions for building features
➡️ Manual review and approval workflows

Total infrastructure cost is basically electricity and API credits. No AWS bill. No Kubernetes cluster. No DevOps team.

The indie hacker playbook usually means "pick one product and go deep." I'm running the opposite experiment. Multiple small bets, shared infrastructure, compounding returns from the monorepo.

Every new site I add gets the existing voice system, SEO pipeline, deployment flow, and CRM integration for free. The marginal cost of launching site #5 is close to zero because the infrastructure already exists.

You don't need a server rack. You don't need cloud spend. Two Macs and good architecture go surprisingly far.
posts.cta: What does your solo builder infrastructure look like? Always curious about other people's setups.

inspired_by.author: Daniel Vassallo
inspired_by.summary: Daniel shares his pivot from Amazon engineer to full-time indie maker, launching micro-SaaS products. He details shipping several small bets publicly, with real revenue breakdowns and advice for career changers entering tech via building. Focuses on low-risk experimentation over big swings.
